message.content:
Below is a practical test catalog, split into **Navigation/Link** and **Responsive/Cross‑device**.  
For each test: what it verifies, WCAG SC (if any), and authoritative source + URL.

---

## 1. Navigation / Link Tests

### 1.1 Broken internal / external link detection

**What it verifies**

- All **links resolve successfully** (no 404/500), both internal and external.
- Error pages for broken URLs are handled appropriately.

**WCAG mapping**

- **2.4.4 Link Purpose (In Context)** – links that lead to errors or irrelevant destinations do not match their stated purpose.[6][7]  
- **3.2.4 Consistent Identification** – links that are labeled the same should behave consistently (e.g., not sometimes broken).[2][6]

**Source**

- WCAG 2.1, SC 2.4.4 “Link Purpose (In Context)” and SC 3.2.4 “Consistent Identification” – W3C.[6][7]  
  URL: `https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG21/` and `https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/Understanding/link-purpose-in-context`  

---

### 1.2 Correct anchor targets (in‑page anchors, section links)

**What it verifies**

- Links with fragment identifiers (e.g., `#section`) **move focus/viewport to the intended section**.
- Target elements exist and have correct IDs.

**WCAG mapping**

- **2.4.1 Bypass Blocks** – in‑page links like “Skip to content” or TOC links let users bypass repeated content.[6]  
- **2.4.4 Link Purpose (In Context)** – link purpose (e.g., “Jump to comments”) is fulfilled by scrolling/focus to the correct section.[6][7]  
- **2.4.3 Focus Order** – focus moves in a meaningful order when using in‑page anchors.[2][6]

**Source**

- WCAG 2.1 SC 2.4.1, 2.4.3, 2.4.4 – W3C.[6]  
  URL: `https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG21/`

---

### 1.3 Breadcrumb correctness

**What it verifies**

- **Breadcrumb trail** reflects the page’s actual hierarchy/location.
- Each breadcrumb item is a working link (except the current page).
- Breadcrumbs are presented consistently across similar pages.

**WCAG mapping**

- **2.4.8 Location (AAA)** – provides information about the user’s location within a set of Web pages.[6]  
- **3.2.3 Consistent Navigation (AA)** – repeated navigation components such as breadcrumbs appear in a consistent relative order.[1][2][6]

**Source**

- WCAG 2.1 SC 2.4.8 “Location”, SC 3.2.3 “Consistent Navigation” – W3C.[6]  
  URL: `https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG21/`  

---

### 1.4 Navigation “active state” / current-page indication

**What it verifies**

- The **current page** is clearly indicated in navigation (e.g., selected tab/menu item) visually and, where appropriate, programmatically (e.g., `aria-current`).
- Only one item is indicated as current within a nav set.

**WCAG mapping**

- **2.4.4 Link Purpose (In Context)** – users understand which navigation element represents the current view.[6][7]  
- **2.4.8 Location (AAA)** – current location in a set of pages is indicated.[6]  
- **1.3.1 Info and Relationships** – programmatic indication (e.g., `aria-current="page"`) conveys the relationship of the current item in the nav structure.[6]

**Source**

- WCAG 2.1, SC 1.3.1, 2.4.4, 2.4.8 – W3C.[6][7]  
  URL: `https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG21/`

---

### 1.5 Redirect chains / canonical link tag

**What it verifies**

- Links do not use excessive **redirect chains** that can confuse assistive tech or impair performance.
- **Canonical link (`<link rel="canonical">`)** correctly indicates the preferred URL, avoiding duplicate content and ambiguous navigation entry points.

**WCAG mapping**

- There is no explicit WCAG SC for SEO‑centric canonical tags or redirect length.  
- Long redirect chains or inconsistent destinations can indirectly affect:
  - **2.4.4 Link Purpose (In Context)** – link destination should match purpose.[6][7]  
  - **3.2.3 Consistent Navigation** – navigation should behave consistently across pages.[1][2][6]

**Source**

- WCAG 2.1 SC 2.4.4, 3.2.3 – W3C.[6][7]  
  URL: `https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG21/`  

*(Note: canonical tags and redirect chains are mainly performance/SEO topics; WCAG references are indirect.)*

---

### 1.6 Hash / deep‑link navigation (direct linking to states/sections)

**What it verifies**

- **Deep links** (routes with fragments, query params, or SPA state) open the correct view/state and place focus appropriately.
- No loss of critical content or functionality when accessed directly.

**WCAG mapping**

- **2.4.1 Bypass Blocks** – deep links can serve as bypass mechanisms to reach specific sections.[6]  
- **2.4.3 Focus Order** – focus order remains meaningful for users starting from a deep link.[2][6]  
- **4.1.2 Name, Role, Value** – dynamic components updated via deep linking maintain correct name, role, and value for assistive technologies.[2][6]

**Source**

- WCAG 2.1 SC 2.4.1, 2.4.3, 4.1.2 – W3C.[2][6]  
  URL: `https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG21/`

---

### 1.7 Browser Back/Forward behavior

**What it verifies**

- Using **Back/Forward** returns the user to the expected state (e.g., scroll position, previously expanded nav state) without losing content or trapping focus.
- No unexpected page reloads that lose form input or navigation context.

**WCAG mapping**

- **3.2.1 On Focus** – components do not change context unexpectedly on focus when navigating back.[6]  
- **3.2.2 On Input** – changing a setting (e.g., selecting a link, form submission) does not unexpectedly change context without user awareness.[6]  
- **2.4.3 Focus Order** – focus order remains logical after using Back/Forward.[2][6]

**Source**

- WCAG 2.1 SC 2.4.3, 3.2.1, 3.2.2 – W3C.[2][6]  
  URL: `https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG21/`

---

### 1.8 404 / error page existence and behavior

**What it verifies**

- A **custom 404 (and other error pages)** exists, is reachable for invalid URLs, and:
  - Provides clear error messaging.
  - Contains navigation (e.g., main menu, search) to recover.
- Page title and heading clearly indicate the error.

**WCAG mapping**

- **2.4.2 Page Titled** – the error page has a descriptive title (e.g., “Page not found”).[6]  
- **2.4.1 Bypass Blocks / 2.4.5 Multiple Ways** – navigation/search enables users to find other content.[6]  
- **3.3.1 Error Identification** – error is clearly identified.[6]

**Source**

- WCAG 2.1 SC 2.4.1, 2.4.2, 2.4.5, 3.3.1 – W3C.[6]  
  URL: `https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG21/`

---

### 1.9 Skip‑to‑content link

**What it verifies**

- Presence of a **“Skip to main content”** (or similar) link at top of page.
- Link is **keyboard‑focusable** and visible when focused.
- Activation moves focus to main content container.

**WCAG mapping**

- **2.4.1 Bypass Blocks (A)** – provides a mechanism to bypass blocks of repeated content.[6]  
- Test method is explicitly described in Section 508 ICT Testing Baseline for SC 2.4.1.[2]

**Source**

- WCAG 2.1 SC 2.4.1 “Bypass Blocks” – W3C.[6]  
  URL: `https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG21/`  
- Section 508 ICT Testing Baseline – “All Baselines Tests for Web.”[2]  
  URL: `https://ictbaseline.access-board.gov/allwebbaselines.html`

---

### 1.10 Consistent navigation structure and naming

**What it verifies**

- Main navigation, footer links, and key navigation widgets:
  - **Appear in a consistent order** across pages.
  - Use **consistent naming** for the same functions/targets.

**WCAG mapping**

- **3.2.3 Consistent Navigation (AA)** – repeated navigation mechanisms occur in the same relative order.[1][2][6]  
- **3.2.4 Consistent Identification (AA)** – components that have the same functionality are identified consistently (e.g., “Home” link always named “Home”).[2][6]

**Source**

- WCAG 2.1 SC 3.2.3 and 3.2.4 – W3C.[6]  
  URL: `https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG21/`  
- Section 508 ICT Testing Baseline tests for SC 3.2.3 and 3.2.4.[2]  
  URL: `https://ictbaseline.access-board.gov/allwebbaselines.html`

---

### 1.11 Keyboard navigation of menus and links

**What it verifies**

- All navigation and links are operable **via keyboard alone**:
  - Tab/Shift+Tab moves focus through items.
  - Enter/Space/Arrow keys activate or move within menus as appropriate.

**WCAG mapping**

- **2.1.1 Keyboard (A)** – all functionality available from a keyboard.[2][6]  
- **2.1.2 No Keyboard Trap (A)** – no keyboard trap in navigation.[2][6]  
- **2.4.3 Focus Order (A)** – focus order preserves meaning.[2][6]

**Source**

- WCAG 2.1 SC 2.1.1, 2.1.2, 2.4.3 – W3C.[2][6]  
  URL: `https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG21/`  
- A11Y Collective – “How to test the basics of keyboard navigation.”[3]  
  URL: `https://www.a11y-collective.com/blog/how-to-test-for-web-accessibility-an-introduction/`

---

### 1.12 Link text clarity and purpose

**What it verifies**

- **Link text** alone, or with its context, conveys the destination or action (no “Click here” without context).
- Same link text is not used for different destinations unless clearly disambiguated.

**WCAG mapping**

- **2.4.4 Link Purpose (In Context) (A)** – purpose of each link can be determined from link text alone or with its context.[6][7]  
- **2.4.9 Link Purpose (Link Only) (AAA)** – for AAA, link purpose from link text alone.[6]

**Source**

- WCAG 2.1 SC 2.4.4 and 2.4.9 – W3C.[6][7]  
  URL: `https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG21/`  
- A11Y Collective – “How to test for clarity of the text displayed in links.”[3]  
  URL: `https://www.a11y-collective.com/blog/how-to-test-for-web-accessibility-an-introduction/`

---

## 2. Responsive / Cross‑Device Tests

### 2.1 Viewport meta tag

**What it verifies**

- `<meta name="viewport">` is present and configured to match device width (e.g., `width=device-width, initial-scale=1`) so layout adapts on mobile devices.

**WCAG mapping**

- **1.4.10 Reflow (AA)** – content can reflow without two‑dimensional scrolling at 320 CSS pixels width.[6]  
- The Understanding document for 1.4.10 notes proper viewport configuration as a common technique for responsive layouts.[6]

**Source**

- WCAG 2.1 SC 1.4.10 “Reflow” – W3C.[6]  
  URL: `https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG21/`

---

### 2.2 Breakpoint coverage (e.g., 320/375, 768, 1024/1440)

**What it verifies**

- At **key viewport widths** (commonly 320 or 375px, 768px, 1024px, 1440px), the layout:
  - Remains usable.
  - Does not introduce hidden/lost content or overlapping controls.
  - Maintains correct reading order.

**WCAG mapping**

- **1.4.10 Reflow (AA)** – specifically requires support at 320 CSS pixels width (equivalent to 1280px at 400% zoom).[6]  
- **1.3.2 Meaningful Sequence (A)** – reading order is preserved across responsive layouts.[6]

**Source**

- WCAG 2.1 SC 1.4.10, 1.3.2 – W3C.[6]  
  URL: `https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG21/`

*(The specific breakpoints 375/768/1024/1440 are industry patterns, not in WCAG; WCAG only specifies the 320 CSS pixel requirement.)*

---

### 2.3 No horizontal scroll at 320px width (and equivalent)

**What it verifies**

- At **320 CSS px** width (or equivalent zoom), content reflows **without horizontal scrolling** for text blocks (except for content explicitly exempted by WCAG such as images, data tables, etc.).

**WCAG mapping**

- **1.4.10 Reflow (AA)** – content can be presented **at a width equivalent to 320 CSS pixels** without two‑dimensional scrolling for vertical text.[6]

**Source**

- WCAG 2.1 SC 1.4.10 “Reflow” – W3C.[6]  
  URL: `https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG21/`

---

### 2.4 Orientation support

**What it verifies**

- Content and functionality are **not restricted to a single display orientation** (portrait or landscape) unless a specific orientation is essential (e.g., piano app, VR).

**WCAG mapping**

- **1.3.4 Orientation (AA)** – content does not restrict its view and operation to a single orientation.[6]

**Source**

- WCAG 2.1 SC 1.3.4 “Orientation” – W3C.[6]  
  URL: `https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG21/`

---

### 2.5 Reflow at 320px (mobile) – including zoom to 400%

**What it verifies**

- At 320 CSS px width **or** at **400% zoom** on 1280px wide content:
  - No two‑dimensional scrolling is required for text content.
  - All content and functionality remain available.

**WCAG mapping**

- **1.4.10 Reflow (AA)** – explicitly defines the 320 CSS px requirement and references the 400% zoom scenario.[6]  
- **1.4.4 Resize Text (AA)** – also interacts with zoom behavior; see next item.[6]

**Source**

- WCAG 2.1 SC 1.4.10 – W3C.[6]  
  URL: `https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG21/`

---

### 2.6 Content reflow and readability on zoom to 200%–400% (text resize)

**What it verifies**

- When users **zoom up to 200% or 400%** (via browser zoom):
  - Text (except captions/images of text) can be resized without assistive tech.
  - No loss of content or functionality.
  - No clipping or overlap that prevents reading or interaction.

**WCAG mapping**

- **1.4.4 Resize Text (AA)** – text (except captions/images of text) can be resized up to 200% without assistive technology and without loss of content or functionality.[6]  
- **1.4.10 Reflow (AA)** – ensures that at high zoom (400% on large screens) content can still be viewed without 2‑D scrolling.[6]

**Source**

- WCAG 2.1 SC 1.4.4 “Resize Text”, SC 1.4.10 “Reflow” – W3C.[6]  
  URL: `https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG21/`  
- USWDS in‑page navigation tests include zoom magnification checks aligned with WCAG 2.1 AA.[4]  
  URL: `https://designsystem.digital.gov/components/in-page-navigation/accessibility-tests/`

---

### 2.7 Text spacing

**What it verifies**

- When user‑applied styles adjust **line height, paragraph spacing, letter spacing, and word spacing** to the values specified by WCAG, all content remains readable and functional (no truncation, overlap, or clipped controls).

**WCAG mapping**

- **1.4.12 Text Spacing (AA)** – no loss of content or functionality when users override text spacing to:
  - Line height: at least 1.5 times font size.
  - Paragraph spacing: at least 2 times font size.
  - Letter spacing: at least 0.12 times font size.
  - Word spacing: at least 0.16 times font size.[6]

**Source**

- WCAG 2.1 SC 1.4.12 “Text Spacing” – W3C.[6]  
  URL: `https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG21/`

---

### 2.8 Touch target size – WCAG 2.5.8 (24×24) and 2.5.5 (44×44)

**What it verifies**

- **Pointer targets** are large enough and not too close together to avoid accidental activation:
  - **WCAG 2.5.8 Target Size (Minimum) (AA)** – minimum target size of **24 by 24 CSS pixels** (with some exceptions) in WCAG 2.2.[6]  
  - **WCAG 2.5.5 Target Size (AAA)** – larger target size of **44 by 44 CSS pixels**.[6]

**WCAG mapping**

- **2.5.5 Target Size (AAA)** – in WCAG 2.1/2.2, targets have a size of at least 44 by 44 CSS pixels.[6]  
- **2.5.8 Target Size (Minimum) (AA)** – added in WCAG 2.2 for 24×24, but referenced here since you explicitly cited it; this is part of WCAG 2.2, not 2.1.[6]

**Source**

- WCAG 2.x SC 2.5.5 “Target Size (Enhanced/AAA)” and 2.5.8 “Target Size (Minimum)” – W3C (see normative text in WCAG 2.2; WCAG 2.1 only includes 2.5.5).[6]  
  URL: `https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG21/` (and corresponding WCAG 2.2 spec for 2.5.8)

*(Note: WCAG 2.1 only defines 2.5.5 (AAA); 2.5.8 is added in WCAG 2.2.)*

---

### 2.9 Apple HIG touch target size (44pt)

**What it verifies**

- On iOS and similar touch devices, **interactive controls** (buttons, links, icons) are at least **44×44 points** to be easily tappable with a finger.

**WCAG mapping**

- No direct WCAG SC, but closely aligned with **2.5.5 Target Size (AAA)** requirements for 44×44 CSS pixels.[6]

**Source**

- Apple Human Interface Guidelines – Apple Inc., which recommends a minimum 44×44 pt hit area for touch controls (authoritative HIG from Apple; URL omitted here per instruction but available from Apple’s developer documentation).  
- WCAG 2.1 SC 2.5.5 “Target Size (AAA)” – W3C.[6]  
  URL: `https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG21/`

---

### 2.10 Responsive navigation behavior (menus, in‑page navigation) at different viewports and zoom

**What it verifies**

- Navigation components (e.g., hamburger menus, in‑page nav) remain:
  - Usable at all supported breakpoints and zoom levels (up to 400%).
  - Keyboard accessible and screen‑reader friendly.
  - Free from overlap/clipping when space is constrained.

**WCAG mapping**

- **1.4.10 Reflow (AA)** – nav components must reflow.[6]  
- **2.1.1 Keyboard (A)** – nav remains keyboard operable.[2][6]  
- **4.1.2 Name, Role, Value (A)** – nav controls expose correct semantics at all sizes (e.g., expanded/collapsed states).[2][6]

**Source**

- WCAG 2.1 SC 1.4.10, 2.1.1, 4.1.2 – W3C.[2][6]  
  URL: `https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG21/`  
- USWDS “In‑page navigation accessibility tests” (includes zoom and keyboard tests aligned to WCAG 2.1 AA).[4]  
  URL: `https://designsystem.digital.gov/components/in-page-navigation/accessibility-tests/`

---

### 2.11 Logical reading order and grouping at all breakpoints

**What it verifies**

- As layout changes responsively:
  - **Reading order** (DOM order) still matches the visual order.
  - Related items remain grouped logically.

**WCAG mapping**

- **1.3.2 Meaningful Sequence (A)** – content presented in a meaningful sequence that preserves its meaning.[6]  
- **1.3.1 Info and Relationships (A)** – relationships conveyed visually (grouping, headings) are also available programmatically.[6]

**Source**

- WCAG 2.1 SC 1.3.1, 1.3.2 – W3C.[6]  
  URL: `https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG21/`  
- A11Y Collective – tests for logical order of content and proximity at 400% zoom.[3]  
  URL: `https://www.a11y-collective.com/blog/how-to-test-for-web-accessibility-an-introduction/`

---

### 2.12 Cross‑device keyboard navigation and focus visibility

**What it verifies**

- On all device sizes:
  - **Visible focus indicator** for interactive components (links, buttons, nav items).
  - No focus loss when layouts switch (e.g., when rotating device or resizing window).

**WCAG mapping**

- **2.4.3 Focus Order (A)** – focus remains logical.[2][6]  
- **2.4.7 Focus Visible (AA)** – keyboard‑operable interfaces must have a visible focus indicator.[6]

**Source**

- WCAG 2.1 SC 2.4.3, 2.4.7 – W3C.[2][6]  
  URL: `https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG21/`  
- Section 508 ICT Testing Baseline tests for focus order.[2]  
  URL: `https://ictbaseline.access-board.gov/allwebbaselines.html`

---

If you want, I can turn this into a tabular checklist (test name | what it verifies | WCAG SC | standard/source URL) ready for a test case management tool.
